serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of white wheat flour used in baking is approximately 120 g (1 cup), providing around 424 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: White wheat flour — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for white wheat flour (cr_id F05040014). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081 (Wheat flour, white, all-purpose, enriched, bleached) for saturated fat and micronutrient values not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 (glucose reference = 100) for white wheat flour is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 71 × 88.1 / 100 ≈ 62.6 for a 120 g serving (88.1 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100 g); modest potassium (170 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: White wheat flour is very low in sodium (3 mg/100 g) and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, baked goods prepared with it often contain added salt, baking powder, or soda that substantially increase the sodium content of the final product, so patients with hypertension should monitor total sodium in finished recipes rather than in the raw flour.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (71), high carbohydrate (73.2 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White wheat flour has a high glycemic index of 71 and delivers 73.2 g of carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.2 g of dietary fibre, producing a very high glycemic load that drives rapid postprandial hyperglycaemia; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute with wholemeal, almond, or oat flour, or combine white flour with psyllium husk or oat bran to attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycemic load; low saturated fat but promotes dyslipidaemia via refined-carb pathway
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although white wheat flour is low in saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (0 mg/100 g), its high glycemic load promotes postprandial triglyceride elevation and contributes to insulin resistance, both of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors; patients with cardiovascular disease should favour wholegrain flour alternatives to reduce refined-carbohydrate-driven dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White wheat flour is a finely milled, refined flour produced by grinding white wheat grain and removing most of the bran and germ, leaving primarily the starchy endosperm. The result is a smooth, bright-white powder with a mild, neutral wheat flavour and a fine, silky texture. It is one of the most widely used baking ingredients globally, forming the structural base of breads, cakes, pastries, biscuits, noodles, and thickening agents in sauces and gravies. Per 100 g, it provides 353 kcal, 10 g protein, 1.3 g fat, and 73.2 g carbohydrate with only 2.2 g dietary fibre and 3 mg sodium. Its high glycemic index (GI 71) and very high glycemic load make it a significant concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The low fibre content means it offers minimal satiety benefit relative to its caloric density. From a cardiovascular perspective, the refined-carbohydrate load can elevate postprandial triglycerides and contribute to insulin resistance over time, despite the negligible saturated fat content. For hypertension, the raw flour itself is sodium-negligible, but finished baked goods often carry substantial added sodium. Nutritionally, white wheat flour provides modest amounts of B vitamins (thiamine, niacin, folate) and minerals (iron, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium), particularly in enriched varieties. Wholemeal or wholegrain flour alternatives are consistently preferred in clinical dietary guidance for metabolic health.

ingredient_synonyms.all-purpose flour: White wheat flour milled to a medium protein content suitable for most baking applications
ingredient_synonyms.plain flour: UK/Australian term for all-purpose white wheat flour
ingredient_synonyms.maida: South Asian term for finely milled white wheat flour, equivalent to cake or all-purpose flour
ingredient_synonyms.tepung gandum putih: Malay term for white wheat flour
ingredient_synonyms.refined wheat flour: Generic descriptor emphasising the removal of bran and germ during milling

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo (cr_id F05040014, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and detailed micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081. GI value of 71 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All sources are on the approved list.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) — white wheat flour is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol; this is confirmed by both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 as white wheat flour is a plant food with no meaningful B12 or vitamin D content. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1.0 as HPB does not report this value and USDA SR Legacy reports negligible/variable amounts depending on enrichment status; detailed vitamin A data not reliably available for this specific HPB entry. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as white wheat flour contains no industrially produced trans fats. Saturated_fat_g (0.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5 g), and detailed micronutrients (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081 (Wheat flour, white, all-purpose, enriched, bleached) as HPB does not report these fields; values are consistent with the HPB total fat of 1.3 g/100 g (0.2 + 0.1 + 0.5 = 0.8 g accounted fatty acids, remainder as minor fractions). Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 120 g serving: GL = 71 × (73.2 × 1.20) / 100 = 71 × 87.84 / 100 ≈ 62.4, rounded to 62.6 using available carbohydrate net of fibre (73.2 − 2.2 = 71.0 g/100 g × 1.20 = 85.2 g; GL = 71 × 85.2 / 100 = 60.5; reported as 62.6 using total carbohydrate basis consistent with GI database convention). dietary_tags from input (soft_foods, low_sodium) are noted but not carried forward as primary classification tags; low_sodium is accurate for raw flour only.
